subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: You need a Go board, which is a wooden or plastic grid of intersecting lines. The standard board is nineteen lines by nineteen lines, but if you are learning for the first time, a nine-by-nine board is much easier to start with, and a thirteen-by-thirteen board is a nice middle ground. You also need one hundred eighty-one black stones and one hundred eighty white stones. These are smooth, flat pieces, usually made of glass or plastic. Each player gets a bowl to hold their stones during the game. If you look at the board closely, you will notice nine small dots placed at specific intersections. These are called star points, and they are simply reference markers to help you orient yourself on the board. They do not affect gameplay in any way.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: Place the board on the table between the two players. The board starts completely empty with no pieces on it at all. Here is the most important thing to understand about the Go board: stones are placed on the intersections where lines cross, not inside the squares. This is different from chess or checkers. On a standard nineteen-by-nineteen board, there are three hundred sixty-one intersections, and every single one of them is a legal place to put a stone. If you are playing your very first game, use a nine-by-nine board instead. It has only eighty-one intersections, so games are much shorter and you can focus on learning the core concepts without feeling overwhelmed.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: One player takes the bowl of black stones and the other takes the bowl of white stones. In Go, Black has the advantage of playing first, so White receives compensation points called komi to balance the game. Komi is usually six and a half or seven and a half points added to White's final score. The half point is intentional because it guarantees there can never be a tie. If two players are not equal in skill, Go has a beautiful handicap system. The weaker player takes Black and places between two and nine stones on the star points before play begins. This head start evens the game out so both players can enjoy a competitive match.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: The board begins empty. In an even game with no handicap, Black places the first stone on any intersection on the board. There are no restrictions on where this first stone can go. After that, players simply alternate turns, Black then White then Black, for the rest of the game. That is all there is to getting started. Pick up a black stone, place it at any intersection, and the game has begun.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: On your turn, you do exactly one thing. You either place a single stone of your color on any empty intersection, or you pass. That is it. There are no other options. Once you place a stone on the board, it never moves. It stays on that exact intersection for the entire game unless your opponent captures it and removes it. The game ends when both players pass one after another. When you feel there are no more useful moves to make, you pass, and if your opponent agrees by also passing, the game is over and you move on to scoring. The entire structure of a turn is just that simple. Place a stone or pass. The extraordinary depth of Go comes not from complicated rules but from the consequences of where you choose to place each stone.

subtopics.title: Capturing, Liberties, and Territory
subtopics.content:
Let's start with liberties, because everything else builds on this idea. Every stone on the board has liberties, which are the empty intersections directly next to it along the grid lines. Think up, down, left, and right, but never diagonally. A stone sitting in the middle of the board has four liberties. A stone on the edge has three. A stone in the corner has only two. When two or more stones of the same color are directly adjacent along the lines, they form a connected group and share all their liberties together.

Now for capturing. When you place a stone that fills the very last liberty of an opponent's stone or group, you capture those stones. Pick them up off the board and set them aside as prisoners. Each prisoner is worth one point at the end of the game. There is one restriction on placement called the suicide rule. You cannot place a stone where it would have zero liberties, unless doing so captures opponent stones first, which would then free up liberties for your stone.

Finally, territory. This is the goal of the entire game. Empty intersections that are completely surrounded by stones of your color count as your territory. Each intersection of territory is worth one point. Your aim is to use your stones to enclose more empty space than your opponent does. You do not need to fill the territory with stones. You just need to wall it off so that only your color surrounds it.

subtopics.title: The Ko Rule and Eyes
subtopics.content:
Sometimes a situation arises where two single stones can capture each other back and forth forever. You take one of theirs, they take one of yours, and the board position repeats endlessly. The ko rule prevents this loop. If your opponent just captured one of your stones in this kind of situation, you cannot immediately recapture on your very next turn. You must play somewhere else on the board first. This move played elsewhere is called a ko threat, because ideally it forces your opponent to respond, allowing you to come back and recapture on the following turn. Ko fights add a fascinating layer of whole-board strategy.

Now for eyes, which are essential to understanding life and death in Go. An eye is an empty intersection that is completely surrounded by stones of one color. Here is the critical rule: a group that has two separate eyes cannot ever be captured. Even if your opponent surrounds the group from the outside and fills every external liberty, they cannot place a stone inside both eyes because doing so would be suicide. A group with two eyes is called alive. A group that is surrounded and cannot form two eyes is dead. Dead groups are removed from the board as prisoners during scoring. Learning to recognize whether a group can form two eyes is one of the most important skills in Go.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
The game ends when both players pass consecutively, meaning neither player believes there is a beneficial move left to play. Before scoring, the players look at the board together and agree on which groups are alive and which are dead. Any dead stones are removed from the board and added to the prisoners.

The most common scoring method internationally is territory scoring. Count one point for each empty intersection of territory you control, then add one point for each prisoner you captured. White also adds komi, usually six and a half or seven and a half points, to compensate for Black going first. The player with the higher total wins. The half point in komi means there is never a tie.

There is also area scoring, used in Chinese rules. Under area scoring, you count one point for every stone you have on the board plus one point for each intersection of territory. Komi still applies. In practice, both scoring methods almost always produce the same winner, so do not worry too much about which one you use as a beginner.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: When the board is empty and you are choosing where to play your first stones, head for the corners. Corners are the most efficient place to claim territory because two edges of the board act as natural walls for you. That means you need far fewer stones to enclose space in a corner than you would in the center. After you have established positions in the corners, expand along the sides of the board, which have one edge working in your favor. The center of the board is the hardest place to claim territory because you have no edges helping you, so save it for last. As a simple guideline, playing on the third line from the edge tends to secure solid, concrete territory. Playing on the fourth line from the edge projects more influence outward across the board. Both are good, and learning when to choose one over the other is part of growing as a player.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
The number one beginner mistake is trying to capture everything. Go is not about destroying your opponent. It is about controlling more territory. If you chase every enemy stone across the board, you will spread yourself thin and leave your own groups weak and vulnerable. Focus on building efficient walls around empty space rather than hunting for captures.

Another common error is ignoring your opponent's threats. When a move threatens one of your groups, think carefully about whether you need to respond right away. A group you could save with one stone now might need three stones to rescue later, or it might die entirely. Reading just one move ahead prevents most disasters.

New players also tend to play too close to their own strong groups. If a group is already alive and secure, building more stones right next to it is wasteful. Play where the open potential is, usually away from settled positions where the board is still being contested.

Finally, never forget about eyes. Every group you build on the board needs the ability to form two eyes if your opponent surrounds it. As the board fills up, check your groups. A large group without two eyes is a large group that can die all at once.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
The central strategic tension in Go is the tradeoff between territory and influence. Playing on the third line, close to the edge, gives you solid territory that is hard for your opponent to take away. But it keeps your reach short. Playing on the fourth line, one step further from the edge, projects influence toward the center and across the board, giving you a framework to build on. The tradeoff is that influence can be invaded. It is potential, not guaranteed points. Neither approach is wrong. The skill is reading the board and deciding what the position needs right now.

One of the most powerful concepts in Go is called tenuki, which means playing somewhere else entirely instead of responding to your opponent's last move. After your opponent plays in one area, you do not have to answer locally. If you see a bigger opportunity on the other side of the board, you can take it. Knowing when a local response is less valuable than a move elsewhere is a whole-board skill that separates stronger players from beginners. Always ask yourself whether the biggest move on the board is here or somewhere else.

metadata.notes: Content rewritten to production quality. Source: AGA Simplified Rules and British Go Association. Voice-friendly prose. Go is deceptively deep — emphasis on making territory and capture crystal clear for beginners.
